title: Pawn Race with King Support
setup: White's king already stands on **e6** — heavily supporting any push. The a-pawn just needs a starting kick.
hint: When your king is active and the pawn is far from promotion, push the pawn — the king is in position to escort it later.
explanation:
## The Idea

This is a fundamental **king-and-pawn endgame** problem. White has a major advantage: the king is already advanced to **e6**, while the pawn sits on a2. The plan is simple — **race the pawn to promotion**.

White plays {{a4}} (the two-square initial push, faster than {{a3}}).

## Why a4 Over a3?

- {{a4}} saves a tempo: the pawn jumps to a4 in one move instead of taking two moves to reach the same square via {{a3}} then {{a4}}.
- This tempo is crucial in races: the closer the pawn is to promoting, the harder it is for the black king to catch.

## Black's Predicament

The black king on **e8** must run all the way to **a8** to catch the pawn — that's seven king moves. The pawn needs five moves to promote ({{a4}}, {{a5}}, {{a6}}, {{a7}}, {{a8=Q}}). Even without support from the white king, the pawn would win the race comfortably.

But here, the white king on e6 is in the **square of the pawn** *already* with respect to the black king's path — it can interfere or support as needed.

## Pattern to Memorize

> **Pawn races** are won by counting tempos. Always push two squares on the first move when the pawn is on its starting rank — that free tempo often decides everything.

The **square of the pawn rule**: imagine a square with the pawn on one corner and the promotion square on the diagonal. If the enemy king is outside that square, the pawn promotes.
